sentences.text: This is my last day.
I did it on Tuesday, and yesterday, and today.
Are we on tape now?
Well, I said it'd be so hard, see, a lot of people can cry.
I cannot cry.
It's--
OK.
Well, one day somebody squealed, and this foreman came in and told my sisters that he knows that they're hiding me here.
Well, if somebody squealed, you-- there's no way out of it.
So, they start begging him, working on him.
Please, this and that.
Well, they persuaded him, he should let you stay.
The man was a German, he was the foreman of the whole the ranch, you know.
And I presented myself to him, and he gave me a job, because in the wartime, people used to steal from the fields.
They used to have tomatoes, potatoes, carrots, all the stuff, you know-- all this stuff went to the-- for the German army.
So, he told he'd give me some distance, where my job is going to be to guard this field.
And we had to change, some of the-- some weeks I had at night, and some weeks we had a dog, he walked around with a German Shepard, and this was the job.
But still, I-- I was a restless.
On the weekends I used to go into the ghetto, bring out some blankets I bought, and leather soles, shoes.
And I came back, and I sold it.
I risked-- I risked my life, but I-- I was-- I always had to do something extra.
Yeah, well definitely.
It's a-- and we worked there for a while, until the beginning-- the beginning-- either the end of '42 or beginning of '43.
And-- [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] it came noticed to the farm, that it's going to Judenrein-- they have to ship them back to the-- everybody to the ghetto, from everywhere.
And we-- there was also on the-- on this farm, there was kibbutz.
Before the war, they used to train them to go to-- to Palestine then, and they used to teach them how to work on the farms, they trained them for the kibbutz.
There was about 400 people.
And the head of the kibbutz was a young lady-- Leah was her name.
And I knew all the people, we worked together.
So we all went to the ghetto, and we we sent to an area where the people were sent out already, to occupy the houses and apartments.
You went into some rooms, the dinner or lunch was on the table, they couldn't even finish it, they were taken out.
And when we stayed there, the phone lines were still working, so we called, we knew that our aunt and uncle in the family is not far from us.
We call them, they said, we shouldn't stay there, we should come.
And my cousin came and took us where they lived on the [NON-ENGLISH], to the same place where I escaped the ghetto, almost, about the 25, 30 feet from there.
And we start working, my sister starts working in the factory, and I started working.
My sisters worked on the shirts, and I worked on the men's clothing-- to alter the-- the gunshot holes from uniforms, pants.
I start working, and every time they picked up a few of us.
If they caught you in the street, they picked you up.
They were-- right across was the Umschlagplatz.
Did you ever hear of what the word means, Umschlagplatz?
OK-- the Umschlagplatz was the place where they shipped all the Jews out, either to Auschwitz, or to Germany, mostly to Treblinka.
So, trains-- a train depot-- right across.
When I looked up from the factory, the window, you could see the Umschlagplatz.
Well, if you interview more people, maybe they'll mention this.

And they made us-- us-- well, at first when they brought us in from the farm, and they placed us in those buildings, there was a small pick-up, a few hundred people, they needed-- every day they needed to fill up the trains.
But those people from the kibbutz weren't going easily, they fought back right away.
There were already organized with some weapons, and they fought back.
Those two sisters that I mentioned, when they saw me at the farm first, they picked them up and sent them to a-- to a camp somewhere.
Well, we were still working, and we were watching through the windows, how they pick people up.
As long as we were at the factory, we were safe.
And one day, a guy-- I sit in my-- we had only one room, we got in-- in one bed.
We had to sleep across the bed, my two sisters and I.
And if we had nothing to do, my aunt and uncle had a big apartment, so we went to sit there before we went to sleep, you know.
And this, about three, four people from the underground comes in to my-- they ring the bell, come into my uncle's place.
And they say you, is you Israel Gromon?
Yeah, Israel Gromon.
And I see the one going from the kibbutz, Feifke, what are you doing here?
He says, shhh, I'll tell you later.
Quiet, I'll tell you later.
They were asking for some money to buy weapons, because my uncle was considered the rich man in the-- in the ghetto.
No, he wouldn't give them any money.
They took one son, the middle one, the second one from the oldest, how would you-- that's how I could describe it.
They blindfolded him, took him away for two weeks, until he brought some money.
When he came back, they asked him, where were you?
He says, I don't know, I was blindfolded all the time.
I don't know where.
The ghetto wasn't big-- in the last days the ghetto was very small.
Anyway, he gave them some money.
Then I met this guy in the street, I say Feifke, what this all about, this?
He says, we have an underground, we're going to fight the Germans.
I know we're not going to win, but we're not going to go anymore.
Say, would you like to join?
I say, if you explain to me, I will join.
He says, don't tell your sisters.
This has to be a secret, because if people know, you know what's going to happen.
Well, they blindfolded me, also, and took me to a place, I also didn't know where I'm going, where I'm coming.
And I was explained, the second-- the second man to Anielewica-- did you hear about the man of Anielewica?
He was the organizer of the Warsaw ghetto uprising.
They gave me a gun, and they explained to me what our duties will be.
That we'll meet once a month.
And if you need something, we could phone.
Not to the organization's bunker, but to a private home and they will relay the message.
And I joined, and we were meeting every month.
We were talking about what's going to happen.
We were stored-- we stored gasoline for Molotov cocktails, to make.
And they knew where I-- where my-- where I live, so they getted some people from the same area, about 16 of us.
They said that this is you area.
If the uprising comes to be, if they're going to ship us out, the first thing you have to do is burn-- there was a factory that made parts for airplanes.
This is your first duty, is to break the windows throw enough Molotov cocktails, this will go.
Anyway, one day, first night of [DRINKS] first Passover-- excuse me, even I get my throat dry.
OK.
The first Passover night, first Seder, the ghetto is surrounded.
Ukraines, there were-- they were the SS, also-- SS surrounded, like every 10 feet, an SS with a machine gun.
We knew this is it.
And they-- they called out, with loudspeakers, we should get in the street.
Well, nobody went out.
Next day, they marched in, and we opened fire.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The guard was right about 10 feet from our house.
We were sitting on the roof.
They thought they marched in like usual, like nothing happened, you know?
We opened fire.
And when we opened fire, a few fell, and they backed up.
The backed out of the ghetto.
They backed out.
In the meantime, a few of us had the order to burn this factory.
They went to burn the factory, the factory went up in flames.
They had to regroup, and we regrouped.
And we got the orders, if tanks come in, we should throw some Molotov cocktails.
Next day, a few tanks appeared at the crossing, and we threw some Molotov cocktails from the roof.
Two tanks blew up, and the Germans escaped through the hatch in fire, you know, they were wearing special suits, you know.
And then the whole thing started, you know?
It took us probably, about, almost two weeks, we were fighting.
One night, we actually-- the older people were sitting in a-- in a bunker.
We only were fighting at night, because in the daytime we didn't have a chance, you see?
One night the sister of my uncle says she left-- she prepared for Passover and left all the food, you could use it here.
You know, they used to make fat, you know, from chicken fat and melt it, you know.
And she had a whole pot there, like a cast iron pot, a big one.
Smelts?
Yeah.
And Hakiva Leifer, he fell on the Jordanian front in Israel.
With him, I went through, from Treblinka all the way through to the liberation.

He was liberated in a different place, and I, a different place-- he went, he got a passport from the [NON-ENGLISH], from another soldier that fell, a Israeli, and that's how they got him into Israel.
We came out, we came out-- Hakiva Leifer and I came out in the same group from Treblinka-- that's-- this is later going on--
Yeah I jumped ahead.
I'm going to come to back to Hakiva Leifer.
We went with Hakiva Leifer to pick up this food in the ghetto.
He was also in the underground with me.
He was a hardheaded person, you know.
He was a few years, about three years older than I was.
We go in-- we lived-- the bunker was [NON-ENGLISH] 5, we had to go to the 13.
But when you walked, there was cracked glass, broken glass from the windows from the shooting.
We walked in socks, not in shoes, but they could hear on the other side, walking, cracking the glass.
So we run across near the-- near the wall-- if they shoot, they cannot hit us, you know?
Unless they threw a hand grenade or something.
And we walked, we ran into her house, and we picked up all kind of food, we put it in bags.
You know, and I carried the cast iron, with the fat in my hand, and something in the other hand, my gun was in my waistband.
And as we walk out of the door, a burst of machine gun.
I remained with my--with only the ear of the pot in my hand.
I could laugh about it now, I was scared stiff, you know.
And we-- we run across right away, against the wall.
We came back, and I kept the handle to show that I had the pot, but I-- there isn't anymore, you know?
And we walked across, and we came in with some food.
We came into the bunker, we told her what happened.
As long as you're back alive, to hell with the food.
When-- then we walked out again.
We wanted to silence this machine gun.
We were 18, but we were always the head of the group.
He says, Hakiva said, we have to Shlomo, we have to go.
We take a couple Molotov cocktails, and throw across the-- the wall.
We have to silence this machine gun.
If not, we haven't got a chance to walk out here.
We went and threw a couple of Molotov cocktails, and we silenced him.
We didn't hear anymore, in this area, we didn't have any shooting anymore.
And then we, after we fought, we fought.
And there was a young kid, about 13 years old, Jewish kid, that was going around and squealing to the Germans-- where the bunkers are.
Every second building had a bunker.
And he-- he came to our bunker.
And the SS came down, and said-- opened-- we had a square, like a-- how do you call it?
Before I start taping, I knew everything.
It covered up the hole, you went under the foundation, it covered up the hole.
Then you shook it a little bit, and the sand covered it, that you couldn't see anything, that something's there, The lid, actually.
OK, I can't do it, I have to-- And he opened the lid, and he said, if you don't come out, we'll throw in a few hand grenades, you'll be buried right here.
So everybody figures, as long as we're alive, we have a chance.
If he throws a few hand grenades, that's all gone.
So we all came out.
They put us against the wall.
But I have track with a machine gun, we figured, that's it.
They didn't kill us inside, they'll give us--
And we stood about a half and hour against the wall, and they looked all over for others.
There was one old man, there was a couple in the bunker.
They came from Israel from a visit from Palestine, then, and they got trapped in the war-- with a beautiful little girl, about seven, eight years old.
She came to visit this old man, the father.
And this, I don't know what his name was, Stroop?
Or, the head of the SS in the ghetto.
I think it was Stroop, the name.
I'm not sure.
He took out a Mauser, a big gun, told him to walk in the backyard, and he shot him.
He had a beard, you know, looks like a religious man.
And those two people also, with their child, went to Treblinka with us.
And they marched us off to the Umschlagplatz.
And they put us-- you know on a station, you have this-- a little house for the-- what do you call, the-- they call, in German they call the station master.
There's-- no, not that.
There's a room where they direct the traffic for shifting the-- the tracks.
There's always-- this little house could hold, maybe, how big is, it I don't know.
I can't even remember-- it was so small.
They stuffed in about 3,000 people.
And at nightfall, the Ukrainian were all drunk.
Even they couldn't take the slaughter, so they were all drunk.
They picked out the teenagers, put them in a window, target practice.
You-- I'll be next, you know?
Only boys.
Boys, some men, who knows?
They put them, they told them to stay in the window, and they shot them between the eyes.
So, my sister said, go in the back of us, we'll cover you around so that they don't see you.
We stayed there till the morning.
In the money, they marched us out, there was a stack like hay, about 10 feet high, with dead people, burning.
They poured gas, put wood on it.
And they put us in the cattle cars.
And about 80 people in a car.
You couldn't turn, just like sardines.
The windows were wired or boarded up. 3,600 people.
This were all-- a lot of people for my city, you know, they were in the bakery business.
And they survived for a while, they worked with the Germans, and-- and-- the minute we came to the first station, we knew we were going Treblinka.
Because we knew the roads.
If you go east, you were going to Auschwitz, or to that other camp.
If you went west, you went to Treblinka.
And we recognized the station, that we're going, the way to Treblinka.
Now, everybody start panicking.
Everybody was in panic, and the-- the-- we were traveling, it was a hot day in April.
And we used to-- some people had some money, so they give a bottle to the Polish people at the station to give them some water, and they threw away the bottle.
They said, you don't need any water, you're going to your death, anyway.
They used to make this was-- used to be the sign, you know?
And we traveled the whole day.
People were getting off their mind.
My cousin, Madeline, she's right in the picture there, cried to her father, I want to live.
And her father called out, [NON-ENGLISH], you know?
He was out of his mind.
One person stuck his head out the window, he was shot through the throat.
He was lying and moaning on the-- on the floor.
People didn't even notice, they didn't pay attention even, to it.
Well we stayed, we came into-- we travelled the whole day, we came in like at dusk, we cam in to-- to Treblinka.
We came into Treblinka.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] The whole train couldn't go in, they had-- had two rails.
So they shifted a half a load, half the cars in first, and the other half was in the other side.
And the minute we came in, we heard the shouts, with dogs, [GERMAN]!
You know?
They panicked the people, that they didn't know what's doing with them.
And I observed already, there was a-- my eyes were going all over.
I saw a-- a chain fence.
I have to think about the words, because my mind is going like a record.
Growing through with shrubbery, all around.
[GERMAN].
They had the goal yet, people was so stupid-- not stupid, there were beat-- beaten down.
They said, give away all the valuables.
There were some Jewish people working there in the camp, they walked around with buckets, to throw in the valuables.
I'm still laughing-- then, my uncle had enough money to give away.
But for guns, he didn't want to give.
And when I left the car, my sisters were actually walking like in a daze.
They didn't walk a straight line.
You see, we were-- you know, if you know you're going to your death, you'll be dead in a-- in a short time.
I saw a line forming in the other side.
They-- they walked, they said to go in the [GERMAN]-- if you learn German, you learn a few words.
[GERMAN], you heard the shouts, [GERMAN].
This was behind the chain link fence.
I stayed in line, they picked out 500 young men.
We were told, we're going to go and clean the Warsaw ghetto.
There were dead people, dead-- soldiers.
And also a lot of rats running around.
Well, we stayed there about two hours until they cleaned out the cars.
You didn't hear a sound anymore. 3,100 people, it took about two hours to get rid of them in the gas chamber.
See, now I'm getting a little bit-- it gets to me.
And they put us back in the cars.
In the morning we cam in from Treblinka back to Lublin-- city of Lublin, it's very close to Majdanek.
And, they got--
No, it's OK.
When he's ready, I'm ready, I told you.
OK.
We came into Lublin, we were told that they already have-- the Germans also make mistake.
If not, God forbid, they would have won the war-- if they wouldn't make mistakes.
We were told that the ghetto has already 500 Greek Jews cleaning.
So, they'll send us into Majdanek.
Not that the-- We came into Majdanek.
It was not a camp, it was a-- a torture chamber, the whole camp.
They also had a gas chamber.
There were fields, there were 1, 2, 3, 4, as I remember, I don't know if there was a 5, also.
They call it a field.
Second field, they called Kanada, because the people worked to sort the clothes that came in from all the-- from all the gas chambers, from the people.
And they sorted it, so they found money, all kind of jewelry, you know.
People took with, they were told they were going to work.
They found francs, that people came from-- from France, transports.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] They, I was sent to the third field.
I came in, and we're sent into horse-- horse barracks.
Nothing, no beds, no nothing.
Just straw sacks on the floor.
And nighttime-- in the morning, you put-- stuck them up against the wall, and at nighttime, you pull them down.
It was not yours, whatever you picked.
And you slept-- slept on a straw sack on the floor.
In the morning they called-- what are they called-- they called us on-- on the field, and put us in, and tried to explain to us, this is not a paradise.
And I knew already, it's no paradise.
You're going to go to work.
What was the work?
First of all, we got a slice of bread and coffee.
Then we marched out.
There were kapos.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]
Let me think.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] There were kapos-- I'll come back.
I-- certain words I forget, but I'll-- they're not very important.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] They picked out a group, and they take us outside the camp.
We carry stones, in the morning, from one side of the field to the other side.
After lunch, you took the same stones, you-- you brought them back to the same place you picked them up.
But this was not all.
There was a kapo-- he was so tall, probably till the ceiling here.
A murderer.
I don't know if he was-- till today, I don't know what nationality he was.
He was a-- he had a club, like a baseball club.
The-- he was hitting blue murder, you know.
Everybody, everybody got a beating.
And some people said it was better at Treblinka already, than this work-- it was torture.
And he had another-- another murderous thing he had, that he kicked a person in the groin.
They picked up-- picked out a person, kicked him in the groin.
When he bend down-- they fell-- he put his foot on the throat, and choked him to death.
This was four before lunch, and four after lunch.
This was going gone for about a week.
And I knew he picks on young men.

He wanted to show, probably, that he's stronger than we were, I was-- we were all strong but-- one day I said-- In the meantime, Hakiva Leifer got himself a job cleaning the-- they call it [GERMAN] in German.
Working in the barrack, giving out the food, cutting the bread.
So I say, Hakiva, you have to take me out of this kommando--you have to take me into work something, because it could be any day now, or any hour, if I go.
I say, you know something, give away the bread, I'll give you more soup.
Give away the bread to somebody else and change to go on this kommando.
I talked to some guys, and one accepted it.
I'll give him my bread, daily bread, and he'll take this kommando, and I'll take his-- I'll go to another group.
Well, he went for me, and I took another group.
What was my group?
They try to break in shoes for the front line, for the soldiers.
New shoes, no socks.
Their feet were bleeding.
And besides this, if you were tall, they hit you over the had with a stick, That the-- that could kill you.
And you walked the whole day.
Could imagine walking around a whole day?
And one day I say no, they're not going to kill me this way.
I got out of the line, and I went--I hid in the barrack a whole day.
When the group, they came to go in to eat, to the barracks, I came out.

As, you know, the people were giving out the bread, they were ahead of the barrack-- they always could cut a-- a thinner slice, and when they cut a thinner slice, there's still half a bread left over.
And you couldn't say anything, who were you going to talk to?
Now, let's stop for a moment so we can--
OK.
